thread.section_title: The Seattle Times The Seattle Times
thread.title: The election is the symptom of a deeper disease
thread.title_full: The election is the symptom of a deeper disease

author: Nicki Blake Chafetz
title: The election is the symptom of a deeper disease
text:
I no longer blame Trump.
He may not even be a racist; he’s more like a conductor of racists. He raised his baton and the white supremacist symphony began to play.
I don’t blame him for calling out the bigots and saying that “the white guy” should win. He is the symptom, not the disease. Why shouldn’t Trump call out to the white supremacists, as he brags how “beautiful” and white his skin is if he stays off the beach and doesn’t tan?
It was a winning strategy; that’s obviously what America wanted.
I don’t blame the Piper, no matter how pied he is. I blame the people who claim they are mesmerized by the Piper.
They are not magically enthralled. He is piping the song they want to hear. In fact, it’s their favorite tune.
I don’t blame the Piper. I blame America. Because racism is baked into our cake.
I am Kentucky slave progeny. Because of slave rape, every now and then a white face pops up in my family. I cannot hate white people. I can’t even have that privilege. Because I AM white people. Furthermore, both of my children look white.
I don’t hate white people. I hate white supremacism.
My father, the Rev. Jacob S. Blake, was an AME Church pastor and a friend of the Rev. Martin Luther King Jr. They, along with French Canadian Universalist Unitarian Pastor Charles Eddis, protested in my hometown until grade schools were integrated. I spoke with Pastor Eddis about six months before he died. He told me that he and my dad stayed awake all night the night of King’s death. The two men thought if any youth violence erupted in Evanston, Ill., that night, they would be able to talk it down.
I got a text from a white friend who grew up poor in Georgia. She told me that she’s planning on sending her 27-year-old daughter to Europe, and hopes to make her the guardian of the young woman’s 8-year-old cousin. She herself intends to stay, since “someone has to stick around for when we have to hide Anne Frank.”
The United States of America we once knew is dead.
But if I — a pastor’s daughter, a former Truman scholar in politics, former Seattle litigator and a graduate of two Christian universities — could advise America of anything at all, I would advise progressive whites to stop worrying about Trump. He is the symptom and not the disease.
Instead, start working on the disease within the hearts of your white friends and families.
